1.title: Keeping culture alive through women's hands
1.name: Joldosheva Guldarika
1.lead: There is a faint smell of wool in the air. She sits in front of a vivid shyrdak that has lived forty years but shows no sign of fading. Red and blue patterns weave into pink and green, each separated by sharp triangular borders. To an unfamiliar eye, it is colour and geometry. To Guldarika, it is something else entirely.
1.bodyMain:
Joldosheva Guldarika is a master craftswoman from Kara-Suu village in At-Bashy, Naryn. She reads these patterns the way others read words.

"It's not just about objects — it holds the wishes and blessings of the person who made it. While making it by hand, you naturally begin to think good thoughts, and in a way, those thoughts and wishes are stitched into it. Every stitch becomes a mother's prayer for her children and grandchildren," she explains.

Then she looks at the shyrdak in front of her, the one made by her mother, and pauses.

"Mine don't compare to hers. Her work was truly meticulous."

Guldarika believes she inherited crafting abilities from her skillful mother, learning from a young age by observing how her elders spun yarn, carded wool, and assembled shyrdak pieces.
1.bodyPhoto1:
Guldarika has been involved in this craft for over thirty years. When she started, she didn't know it would become the main source of income for her family. It began simply, from interest and passion. After her husband passed away, she raised her children, married off her daughters, and prepared their dowries.

"Providing for the family, supporting the children — all thanks to this craft.
It feels like a gift."

She also created work for other women in her village, distributing stitching work among them. In villages, there aren't many other ways to earn — no big fields, no steady jobs. So for many local women, shyrdak-making has become the main opportunity.
1.bodyPhoto2:
For Guldarika, craft was never only about income. She speaks of what it once meant to make shyrdak together.

"It wasn't just about the craft — it strengthened relationships. Neighbors and relatives would gather, work together, share advice, and teach each other."

Now that the collective process has quietly faded. But back then, it was all about cooperation, building unity and harmony.
1.bodyPhoto3:
Craft carries cultural meaning in it as well. It conveys Kyrgyz traditions that are essential. Guldarika believes these traditions must be kept alive and passed down, not through preservation from a distance but through example.

"Traditions are preserved only if we live them — I try to embody what I learned from my elders, and in turn, I pass it on to my children and daughters-in-law. That's how culture survives — from one generation's example to the next."

As many other craftswomen, she has been doing exactly that, through every pattern she draws, every woman she teaches, every shyrdak that leaves her hands carrying someone's name in its stitches.
1.photo3FeaturedQuote: The important thing is to keep creating. To keep our culture alive through women's hands.
1.contactText: Interested in Guldarika's work or looking to collaborate? You are welcome to reach out directly:

2.title: "Every Handmade Piece is a Letter to the Next Generation"
2.name: Toktosunova Kenjekan
2.lead:
The shore is empty, the water steel-blue and restless, the sky pale and wide. The air coming off the Ysyk-Kol lake is cool and clean, the kind that clears your head the moment you step outside. This is Tamchy in autumn. 
A few meters from the water, close enough to feel its presence, is Kenjekan's home.  This is where she creates. This is where her colours come from.
2.bodyMain: Toktosunova Kenjekan can not be described by one thing: she is a craftswoman, trainer, producer, and grower of things. Her reputation in the crafts field is serious, having developed thirty-seven products for the global market, and over eight thousand people have been trained by her. In person, she is anything but formal. She welcomes you like family, talks like she has known you for years, and somehow makes you feel that everything is going to be fine.
2.bodyPhoto1:
She is the youngest of twenty-five children, born into a family where craft was a way of living. Her grandfather began working as a craftsman in 1864, making everything nomadic life required. By the 1880s the family was trading along the Silk Road, between Andijan, Urumqi, and Kashgar. Through the Urkun, through Soviet rule, through everything history threw at them, the family and the craft survived. 

"For some people this might seem fascinating. For me it's just everyday life," she says. 
Kenjekan cut her first shyrdak pattern when she was only twelve and sliced her hand down to the base of the fingernail. Her mother looked at the wound and smiled: *"You cut your finger deep –  it means you'll become a famous craftswoman."* 

Her classroom was her mother's hands: 

"I used to watch carefully how my mother worked — how she laid out the pattern –  and whenever I saw a mistake, I would point it out and fix it."
2.bodyPhoto2:
For Kenjekan, a finished piece is never just an object. *"Every handmade piece is a letter to the next generation."* And when you hold one, she believes, you can feel who made it. 

"When you look at it closely, you can feel how much energy and love went into it, what she thought and felt while sewing. You can almost sense her spirit relaxing, enjoying, putting good intentions into each stitch."

When asked where she finds inspiration for her color combinations, she stands up, walks to the door, and opens it. 

"Look, the yellowing colors of the pear tree. The sky, when clouds cover it. The green grass over there. When you look at all this, the colors come to you by themselves."
  
"I just step outside."
2.bodyPhoto3:
When she started working beyond her family, she met Dinara Chochunbaeva. Together, they created a talent-support fund and the first orders came. She evaluated each one carefully, what her team could produce, what they couldn't. After some time, she secured her first independent loan and started expanding from there.

"That allowed me to develop independently."

Today, twenty-five local people work in her production. Every machine owned outright, bought from daily earnings she set aside. Nowadays, the daily management of the production has passed to her son, but she still supervises each process.
2.text:
Away from the orders and the workshop, there is another side of her.
Someone once asked her why she seemed so happy. She didn't hesitate.

"Because I breathe fresh air. I drink tea made from medicinal herbs that grow right here on the land. I drink clean water. Every day I see the sun, and I thank God that I'm alive on this earth."

At night, she looks at the stars. *"I travel among them,"* she says. *"I exercise my eyes that way. And that restores my vision."*
  
She is not finished. Not even close. *"I still have about 46 more years of life,"* she says. 

"If you set a goal, a person can achieve it. Living without goals is not really living."
2.contactText: Interested in Kenjekan's work or looking to collaborate? You are welcome to reach out directly:

3.title: Crafting and Lighting a Way Forward
3.name: Moldalieva Nazira
3.lead:
A blue metal container stands in the yard. Nothing about it announces itself, the kind you see in many courtyards here, unremarkable, easy to walk past. But what is inside tells a different story. Floor to ceiling, colour. Toshoks, juurkans, jazdyks – stacked in towers, each one folded tight, one on top of another. Every item is made by hand. 
Nazira built all of this. And as she will tell herself, she started from scratch.
3.bodyMain:
Nazira Moldalieva is an entrepreneur, craftswoman, and founder of a public foundation in Naryn. But when she started, she knew none of this was coming. Unlike most craftswomen here, craft was not something she grew up with. 

"When I got married, I didn't even know how to thread a needle."

She describes coming to the craft out of necessity when her family faced difficulties.

"In order to feed my children, I did any work I could find. To provide for my family, I had to learn everything, even if I didn't know it at first."

She doesn't look back at those years with bitterness.
  *"Without those days, I wouldn't be where I am today,"* she says.
3.bodyPhoto1:
She started small: sheets, pillowcases, whatever she could manage at home. Slowly, it became something more. 

"Gradually, I discovered that if you have a desire and determination, you can learn anything."

Quality became her personal standard.

"I focus on quality over money. Every piece is made carefully, finished, and tested. I handle every detail with love and care."

Her work started expanding. Today, Nazira employs other women, making sure the work fits around their lives. 

"The women I train are mostly single mothers or mothers with many children. I give them frames and tools, they work at home while the children are asleep, and bring the finished pieces to me."
3.bodyPhoto2:
"I realized that to help someone, it's not necessary to live in a mansion or drive fancy cars. If your heart is rich, that alone is enough." 

On that belief, Nazira founded the SHAAUN-Kaniet public foundation. From her monthly earnings, she sets aside a portion to help families that face financial difficulties, especially single-parent families. 
A few times a month, she gathers women over tea, just to sit, talk, and remind each other that difficult days will pass. 

"Sometimes I feel that God made me like a small lamp – he sends these people to me because perhaps I can give them support or light a way forward."
3.bodyPhoto3:
For Nazira, this is still just the beginning. She has a plan to build a place where women can come together, learn, create, and support each other. A place, she believes, that could bring changes.

"If creative industries for women were developed here, it could change not only individual lives but the entire community."

There is no doubt in her voice when she says it. 

"I hope to become a person who creates opportunities for others and helps new generations to continue this work."
3.contactText: Interested in Nazira's work or looking to collaborate? You are welcome to reach out directly:

4.title: Every Stitch, With Love
4.name: Asanbaeva Ryskul
4.lead: There is a deep pink mannequin in the workshop, standing steady against the white wall. She calls her Natashka. Every morning, when the sun just hits the blue window, Ryskul comes in and turns to her first. They talk a little, dance a little. Only after this does she sit down, and the sewing begins.
4.bodyMain:
Asanbaeva Ryskul is a designer, tailor, and entrepreneur from Kara-Suu village in At-Bashy, Naryn. She has been in this craft for thirty-five years. 

"As far back as I can remember, I have been sewing since childhood."

She always wanted to study design. Instead, she studied medicine, worked as a nurse, and sewed on the side. 

"I balanced my medical job with sewing to earn extra income."

The design work never stopped pulling at her. Eventually, it won.
4.bodyPhoto1:
She started small, setting up a little wagon workshop with a few people and a sewing machine. She felt the need to learn more and went for an internship in Russia. 

"When we went to Russia, they were actually surprised by our skills."

Back home, one seamstress could produce a full garment independently from start to finish. But in Russia, everything ran on a conveyor system.
Ryskul brought back what was most useful, and she came back and kept going. Her husband built a workshop at home. The work expanded into patchworks, quilts, ethnic bags, and traditional ceremonial clothing.

"I mastered many elements of Kyrgyz ethnic design and began applying them in my work."

Today, women work with her, most of them from home, young mothers sewing between school runs and bedtime.
4.bodyPhoto2:
She keeps up with the times. When she sees a new trend on TikTok, her first thought is: we can make this before it becomes common everywhere.

"Fashion changes fast. We have to keep up with the times."

But her best ideas come from closer to home.

"I once designed a dress for my daughter's graduation entirely on my own. When I wore it to her final school ceremony, one of my clients saw it and said, 'Wow, let's produce this model – it's beautiful!' Now I am planning to release that design in spring. That is how new collections often begin."
4.bodyPhoto3:
For her, sewing is never just work.

"I honestly cannot imagine a day without sewing. Even if I am sick, once I start sewing I feel better – it is like therapy for me."

She has a clear view of who belongs in this profession and who doesn't.

"The first type works with soul and passion, for them money is not the main motivation. 
The second type wants to sew but it does not always work out. 
The third type sews simply because they need money,  for such people the work feels heavy, even if the model is simple."
4.bodyPhoto4:
Her love for sewing shows up in the details.

"When I start sewing a new design, I imagine the person who will wear it. Especially when I add ornaments – each pattern has its own symbolic meaning. When elderly women come and tell me how many children they have, I sometimes incorporate horn motifs or other symbols into the design to represent their family and generations."

She wants others to find that same love. 
Nazira organized a school for one month, teaching everything from the very beginning. She believes the interest in the craft among young people is there. What is missing is not desire but guidance.

"My goal is to keep developing and expanding – ideally to turn it into a real factory someday, even if it remains in the village."
4.bodyPhoto5:
Her mother-in-law has watched all of this from the beginning. She speaks quietly.

"She started with one machine. Step by step she learned more, new opportunities opened, she bought better machines, completed courses, received a diploma. After that her work really developed. Now she provides jobs for many women in the village. She supports not only her own family but also helps others earn a living."

No government assistance. Everything bought with her own money, built with her own hands.

"When a person has skilled hands, they can live well anywhere. Thank God, we have enough food and clothing. We live decently."

Ryskul, back at her sewing machine:

"When you truly enjoy what you do, even complex tasks become easier. That is why I believe any profession should be done with love."
4.contactText: Interested in Ryskul's work or looking to collaborate? You are welcome to reach out directly:

5.title: Learn. Create. Listen to Your Heart
5.name: Mustapaeva Nurgul
5.lead:
On the edge of Naryn, there is a building sitting quietly against the mountains. The land around it was once swampy, unusable – given to them because no one else wanted it. They arranged the water, arranged the electricity, and began.
This is where Nurgul builds.
5.bodyMain:
Mustapaeva Nurgul is an entrepreneur and creator from Naryn. Together with her husband, she has been running a business in wool processing and feltmaking for almost twenty years. She trained as a teacher but hardly worked in that profession.

"Something kept drawing me toward this path. I wanted to find my own way."

Her mother and grandmother were skilled craftswomen. She believes the passion passed through them to her. Now, alongside her husband, she processes wool, produces felt for yurts and shyrdak carpets, and supplies nearly the entire Ysyk-Kol region with the felt they need.
5.bodyPhoto1:
Through her work, Nurgul became involved with the Craftsmen's Council of Kyrgyzstan, an association uniting artisans from all seven regions of the country. She serves as the coordinator for the Naryn region, connecting and supporting over two hundred craftswomen across the area.

"Officially, the title is closer to 'vice-president,' but I prefer to call myself a coordinator."

The work is voluntary. But through it she has built connections, met influential figures in the cultural sphere, and helped organize the Shyrdak Festival – now in its fifteenth year.

"We share each other's success and support one another."
5.bodyPhoto2:
In 2022 she traveled to South Korea as part of a cultural cooperation program. She came back as something more certain.

"I returned feeling incredibly proud to be Kyrgyz. I realized that our craft traditions are truly unique and cannot really be compared to those of other countries, and that realization brought me great joy."

The connections kept growing.

"Through craft, I organize events, meet new people, build relationships, and constantly expand my network. I find it genuinely interesting and fulfilling."
5.bodyPhoto3:
She is also looking forward, studying etno-blogging, learning how to film, how to create content, how to bring Kyrgyz craft culture online.

The urgency behind it is real.

"The older generation of mothers and grandmothers is gradually disappearing, and that is one of the reasons we try so hard to learn while we still can. This is what we call heritage – something passed from mother to daughter. The knowledge lives within you. It is like an inner awareness or memory that stays in your consciousness."

"Knowledge itself is endless, and the depth of craft knowledge is also endless. There is always more to learn and develop."
5.bodyPhoto4:
Underneath everything, she has a clear sense of what drives her.

"I believe everyone carries a calling in their heart. In that sense, handicraft is a gift from God."

She speaks about women carrying particular weight.

"Any woman carries a great responsibility. What looks like 'ordinary' daily work – cooking, caring for children, looking after elders, managing the household – actually requires constant effort. The same applies to women engaged in crafts. They organize their time, feed their children, manage their homes, and still create."

To women who want to start but feel uncertain, she has one thing to say:

"It is never too late – the most important thing is to listen to your heart."
5.contactText: Interested in Nurgul's work or looking to collaborate? You are welcome to reach out directly:
